content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95814 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

March 10, 1978

98-2/2

Senator H. L. Richardson
State Capitol
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Senator Richardson:

Thank you for your letter of March 23, 1978, in which you ask whether or not it is permissible for a state agency to take a stand on a ballot initiative and whether a press release announcing its position can be issued by the state agency.

The Political Reform Act does not regulate the question of whether a state agency can take a stand on ballot measures or issue press releases reflecting that position.

The Act regulates disclosure of money being spent for or against measures or candidates. If a person (defined very broadly in Government Code Section 82047) makes expenditures of $500 or more, that person must file a campaign statement. However, a Superior Court recently ruled that the state is not a person within the meaning of the Political Reform Act, and thus the court said that expenditures by the state for election purposes are not reportable. We are currently in the process of appealing that decision.

Our answer to you is limited strictly to the provisions of the Political Reform Act. You may wish to consult with the Attorney General's office with regard to other provisions of law. In doing so, you may wish to refer to Stanford v. Matt, 17 Cal. 3d 206, in which the Supreme Court held that the Director of Parks and Recreation could not make public expenditures to promote a bond measure.

Senator H. L. Richardson
Page Two

March 30, 1978


Please feel free to call on me if you have any
further questions or comments.

Sincerely,

[illegible signature]

Robert M. Stern
General Counsel

RMS:mc

STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814
(916) 445-3684

DISTRICT OFFICE
735 W. DUARTE ROAD, SUITE 207
ARCADIA, CALIFORNIA 91006
(213) 445-4404

[SEAL]

California State Senate

COMMITTEES
ELECTIONS AND REAPPORTIONMENT
NATURAL RESOURCES AND WILDLIFE
REVENUES AND TAXATION

MEMBER
SELECT COMMITTEE ON
PENAL INSTITUTIONS

H. L. "BILL" RICHARDSON
STATE SENATOR
TWENTY-FIFTH DISTRICT

March 23, 1978

Mr. Robert Stern, General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Stern:

Enclosed please find a copy of a press release on Health
and Welfare Agency's stationary from the Commission on
Aging. I would like to know whether or not it is permissible:

1. For a state agency or state commission to take
   an official stand on a ballot initiative; or

2. For a member of such agency or commission to
   issue in his official capacity a release such as
   the enclosed and to use state material, staff,
   time and other resources for such purposes.

A prompt response will be appreciated.

Sincerely,

H. L. "BILL" RICHARDSON

HLR:dmc

Enclosure

HEALTH AND WELFARE AGENCY
California Department of Aging
918 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Contact: Boone Robinson (916) 322-5630

March 6, 1978

No. CCOA-78-2

FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

"Jarvis-Gann is a fraud on the older people of California," said Boone

Robinson, Executive Secretary, California Commission on Aging, in explaining the

Commission's decision to oppose the Jarvis-Gann property tax initiative.

"It is like the old shell game. They give it to you with one hand, and then

take it away with the other. Before you know what hit you, you've lost your entire

stake. I just don't think the people of California will fall for this kind of con

game", continued Robinson.

Action opposing the initiative was taken by the Commission at its regular

monthly meeting March 2 in Sacramento. The Commission is a 25-member advisory body

to the Governor, the Legislature, and the California Department of Aging.

Robinson stated, "Passage of Jarvis-Gann will destroy social services in

California and the elderly will be hurt the most. The increase in sales and income

taxes that must be levied just to maintain essential services will affect those who

can least afford it - California's elderly.

"Everyone should re-examine Jarvis-Gann and learn the truth about the total

effect of this initiative on their own lives."

#

The Political Reform Act does not regulate the question of whether a state agency can take a stand on ballot measures or issue press releases reflecting that position. The Act regulates disclosure of money being spent for or against measures or candidates. If a person (defined very broadly in Government Code Section 82047) makes expenditures of $500 or more, that person must file a campaign statement.
